coredata.dc:title: Effects of a novel carbon nanomaterial on hydration, mechanics, and chloride binding of cement composites
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Wu-Jian
Zhong, An-Nan
Zheng, Shu-Yi
He, Chuang
coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials have made significant progress in the field of cementitious materials, but suffer from primary problems of high cost and poor dispersity. Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel low-cost and high-dispersity nanomaterial preliminarily demonstrate their potential for cement. Nevertheless, it lacks systematic research on their effects on cement properties. For the first time, this work comprehensively investigates the effects of CDs on the hydration, mechanics, and chloride binding of cement composites. More importantly, the relevant mechanisms are revealed in depth. Specifically, cost-efficient ($0.013/g) and well-dispersed (dispersed in simulated concrete pore solution for 7 d) CDs are directly prepared by a facile microwave approach. The CDs dramatically retard the early hydration, improve later mechanical performance, and obviously enhance chloride binding. In detail, 0.2 wt% CDs reduce the second exothermic peak value by 75%, but improve the chloride binding capacity of paste by 51%. And 0.1 wt% CDs enhance the compressive/flexural strength of mortar by 17%/21%. Crucially, related influence mechanisms are uncovered. Namely, CDs create a protective barrier to retard hydration through the complexation of their abundant surface groups with Ca2+; While CDs exert the nucleation effect to boost the formation of C–S–H and Friedel's salt, thereby playing a positive impact on chloride binding; Meanwhile, CDs with suitable dosages carry out nucleation effect to counteract their air-entraining effect, thus improving later mechanical properties of cement composites. This study is expected to provide a novel low-cost and high-dispersity CDs nanomaterial to advance the development of high-performance cementitious materials.

Chemical compositions and surface groups of CDs: (a) FTIR spectra; (b) full range XPS spectra; (c)–(e) XPS spectra with high resolution for C1s, N1s, and O1s.
Optical properties of CDs: (a) UV–vis spectra of CDs (with pictures shot in daylight and a 365 nm UV light); (b) maximum PL spectra of CDs in aqueous solution.
UV–vis and maximum PL spectra of CDs-containing simulated concrete pore solution: (a–b) at different CDs concentrations; (c–d) before and after 7-day sitting.
Hydration of diverse cement pastes: (a) cumulative heat release curves; (b) heat flow curves.
Mechanical properties of various cement mortars at different curing periods: (a) compressive strength; (b) flexural strength.
(a) C b values of various groups at varying immersion times in 3 mol/L NaCl solution; (b) the increase rates of C b from CDs-containing samples compared with C0 group at diverse immersion times.

XRD patterns of diverse cement pastes: (a) after 28-day curing; (b) after 28-day immersion in NaCl solution; The content of different components in diverse cement pastes obtained by XRD-Rietveld method: (c) after 28-day curing; (d) after 28-day immersion in NaCl solution.
DTG curves of each cement paste: (a) after 28-day curing; (b) after 28-day immersion in NaCl solution.
Typical SEM images of various cement pastes: (a)–(c) C0, C2, C4 after 28-day curing; (d)–(f) C0, C2, C4 after 28-day immersion in the NaCl solution.
Pore structure of various cement mortars: (a) cumulative porosity; (b) pore size distribution.
Schematic of mechanism for CDs-induced hydration kinetics retardation.
Schematic of mechanism for CDs affecting mechanical properties.
Bound chloride content distribution in different hydration products (C–S–H, Fs).
Schematic of mechanism for CDs improving chloride binding.

head.ce:title: Effects of a novel carbon nanomaterial on hydration, mechanics, and chloride binding of cement composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials have made significant progress in the field of cementitious materials, but suffer from primary problems of high cost and poor dispersity. Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel low-cost and high-dispersity nanomaterial preliminarily demonstrate their potential for cement. Nevertheless, it lacks systematic research on their effects on cement properties. For the first time, this work comprehensively investigates the effects of CDs on the hydration, mechanics, and chloride binding of cement composites. More importantly, the relevant mechanisms are revealed in depth. Specifically, cost-efficient ($0.013/g) and well-dispersed (dispersed in simulated concrete pore solution for 7 d) CDs are directly prepared by a facile microwave approach. The CDs dramatically retard the early hydration, improve later mechanical performance, and obviously enhance chloride binding. In detail, 0.2 wt% CDs reduce the second exothermic peak value by 75%, but improve the chloride binding capacity of paste by 51%. And 0.1 wt% CDs enhance the compressive/flexural strength of mortar by 17%/21%. Crucially, related influence mechanisms are uncovered. Namely, CDs create a protective barrier to retard hydration through the complexation of their abundant surface groups with Ca2+; While CDs exert the nucleation effect to boost the formation of C–S–H and Friedel's salt, thereby playing a positive impact on chloride binding; Meanwhile, CDs with suitable dosages carry out nucleation effect to counteract their air-entraining effect, thus improving later mechanical properties of cement composites. This study is expected to provide a novel low-cost and high-dispersity CDs nanomaterial to advance the development of high-performance cementitious materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Cementitious materials, the highest consumed construction materials for large construction projects, present numerous benefits involving affordable price, easy processing, pleasurable efficiency, and superior structural properties [1,2]. Nonetheless, on account of the rapid development of large-scale and high-rise engineering structures, the requirements for the mechanical properties and durability of cementitious materials are becoming increasingly stringent. Fortunately, nano-engineering (i.e. incorporating nanomaterials into cementitious materials to improve the microstructure of the cement matrices) promises to become an effective measure to tackle these challenges. Specifically, nanomaterials are able to refine the pore structures of cement matrices, and act as nucleation sites to generate more hydration products, thus leading to higher strength and durability [3]. Up to now, nano-SiO2 [4], nano-TiO2 [5], nano-Al2O3 [6], layered double hydroxides [7–9], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [10], and graphene oxide (GO) [11–13] have been employed in nano-engineering.

Despite enormous advances in the application of nano-engineering in cementitious materials, there are still two primary problems containing high cost and poor dispersity to seriously hinder its large-scale application [14]. For one thing, large-scale preparation of nanomaterials is limited due to their complex preparation methods. New methods have been developed to prepare nanomaterials, but are still time-consuming, and employ expensive raw materials, making nanomaterials remain expensive. For example, ultrasonic method with simple procedures was used to prepare GO, but suffered from long preparation time (more than 2.5 h) and high-cost raw material (i.e., graphite oxide) [15]. Up to now, almost nanomaterials used in cement systems are still not cost-effective. Regarding the most widely applied GO, its price in a reagent procurement platform (https://mall.shiyanjia.com/) is up to more than $273/g. For the construction industry consuming a large quantity of cement, although the dosage of nanomaterials incorporated into cement is relatively low (e.g., for GO, its dosage is generally limited at 0.2 wt%), the investment of nano-modified cement employed in the construction industry is still expensive. This undoubtedly hinders the extensive application of nanomaterials in civil engineering practice. For another thing, the significant specific surface area and elevated interfacial energy of nanomaterials result in formidable van der Waals forces among individual particles. This renders nanomaterials severely agglomerated in cement matrices with high pH and abundant alkali metal ion content, drastically compromising their enhancement effects by occurrence of stress concentration and exacerbation of rheological behaviors [16]. More seriously, to counteract the negative influence of agglomeration, compensatory incorporation of nanomaterials is required, further rising the cost [4]. Various physical and chemical dispersion methods such as mechanical blending [17], ultrasonic dispersion [18], surface modification [19], and surfactant addition [20] have been employed to improve the dispersity of nanomaterials. For instance, ultrasonic approach can disperse nanomaterials [18], but cannot prevent their reaggregation; Surface modification by grafting functional groups overcomes the agglomeration of nanomaterials to some extent [19], but increases the operation complexity and cost. In a word, it still lacks a suitable method to surmount the high cost or poor dispersity barrier of nanomaterials, not to mention simultaneously solving these two problems. If a high-dispersion and low-cost nanomaterial, directly synthesized using a simple and eco-friendly approach, plays a positive effect on cement matrices, it will provide an ideal and efficient solution to address the above problems, thereby promoting the advancement of nano-engineering.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a rising star of carbon-based nanomaterials, have displayed outstanding merits in terms of dispersion stability, ease of preparation, low cost, and eco-friendliness [21–25]. In general, CDs are defined as small carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size and fluorescence as instinct performance [26–28]. CDs contain carbon cores with a significant amount of O-/N-containing functional groups on their surface [27]. CDs can be categorized according to their structures: carbon nanodots, carbon polymer dots, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [29]. Benefiting from their exceptional optical properties, remarkable biocompatibility, appealing catalytic performance, nontoxicity, and environmental friendliness, CDs have found broad application in various fields such as biomedicine, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, lubrication, and corrosion protection [29–34]. Exhilaratingly, CDs have been preliminarily deployed in cementitious materials and demonstrated a promising prospect in anti-chloride corrosion [35,36]. More specifically, in our previous work, GQDs with outstanding dispersity were used to enhance chloride binding of cement pastes. The results illustrated that 0.2 wt% GQDs could improve the chloride binding capacity by 109% [35]. Nevertheless, GQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method goes a time-consuming and energy-consuming process. At the same time, the precursor of nitronaphthalene, obtained by the reaction of naphthalene with concentrated nitric acid for 8 h, are toxic, relatively expensive, and uneasy to obtain. Thus, GQDs in our reported work fail to make full use of CDs advantages of easy preparation, low cost, and eco-friendliness. More importantly, to the best of our knowledge, there are no relevant studies investigating the effect of CDs on other properties of cement composites, not to mention further revealing their influencing mechanisms. Consequently, it is urgently necessary to develop a facile, rapid, environmental-friendly, and low-cost method to prepare high-dispersion CDs, and systematically investigate their effects on the properties of cementitious materials as well as rationally disclose related mechanisms.

In light of this, this study deliberately synthesizes a novel cost-efficient ($0.013/g) and well-dispersed (stably dispersed in simulated concrete pore solutions for 7 d) CDs by a simple, time-saving, and eco-friendly microwave route only using non-toxic citric acid and urea as precursors. And the influence of CDs on hydration, mechanics, and chloride binding of cement composites is comprehensively studied. More importantly, the related influence mechanisms of CDs are deeply uncovered for the first time. Specifically, the morphology, structure, chemical composition, and surface groups of the as-synthesized CDs are determined, and their dispersion stability within simulated concrete pore solution (i.e., saturated Ca(OH)2 solution) is substantiated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The hydration, mechanics, and chloride binding abilities of CDs-containing cement materials are systematically investigated by isothermal calorimetry, compressive/flexural strength tests, and equilibrium method, respectively. And the related mechanisms are reasonably proposed according to the phase composition and microstructure analyses. This work firstly provides low-cost and high-dispersion CDs to improve the properties of cement composites. It would contribute to overcoming intractable problems of existing nanomaterials, thus stimulating the progress of nano-engineering.

Through TEM/HRTEM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, XPS, UV–vis, and PL analysis, the morphology, structure, chemical compositions, surface groups, and optical property of as-prepared CDs are comprehensively described.

The size distribution and morphology of CDs are identified through TEM and HRTEM images. TEM image (Fig. 1 a) unveils that CDs are spherical, and monodisperse particles. CDs exhibit a size distribution ranging from 0.4 to 2.2 nm with an average size of ca. 1.24 nm via randomly counting 120 individual CDs particles (Fig. 1a). HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) shows that the lattice fringes of CDs are well-resolved, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, coinciding with the graphene (100) plane. This suggests that CDs own a favorable crystalline structure. XRD patterns and Raman spectra are applied to validate the structure of the as-synthesized CDs. XRD patterns (Fig. 1c) display a broad peak with the center at 18.5° (reflecting a lattice spacing of 0.49 nm). This is larger than the lattice spacing of graphite (002) plane (0.344 nm), indicating the existence of polymer structure and/or some amorphous carbon/heteroatoms within the carbon framework of CDs [37,38]. As shown in Raman spectra (Fig. 1d), two distinct peaks at 1349 and 1581 cm−1 are ascribed to D peak (disordered defects in sp3) and G peak (sp2-carbon). Moreover, the intensity ratio of D peak and G peak (I D/I G) is 0.75. This illustrates that CDs have a high crystallinity, in agreement with the observation of TEM. The aforementioned analyses present that the as-synthesized CDs possess a monodispersity with a small average diameter of 1.24 nm and a favorable crystalline structure.

The exhaustive information about the chemical compositions and surface groups of CDs are uncovered by FTIR and XPS spectra (Fig. 2 ). Apparently, the wide absorption bands at 3100–3500 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [39]. The peaks at 1716, 1620, and 1427 cm−1 arise from CO (Amide Ⅰ), C–N–H (Amide Ⅱ), and C–N (Amide Ⅲ), respectively [40,41]. Besides, the peaks at 1193 and 1089 cm−1 can be attributed to C–O stretching and C–O–C bending modes [40]. These N- and O-containing groups endow CDs with superior dispersity in aqueous systems. XPS spectra (Fig. 2b–e) support FTIR results. As illustrated in full-range XPS spectra (Fig. 2b), three distinct peaks corresponding to C1s (284 eV), O1s (532 eV), and N1s (400 eV), indicate the only existence of C, O, and N elements in CDs. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), three peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.6 eV are related to CC/C–C, C–O/C–N, and CN/CO, respectively [21]. Apparently, the CC/C–C bond with a dominant content of 71.02% demonstrates that CDs contain abundant sp2-carbon structure, in accordance with the results from TEM and Raman spectra. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be deconvolved into three peaks: –NH2 (399.3 eV), pyrrole-N (400.3 eV), and graphite-N (401.8 eV) [42,43]. And the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) reveals two peaks of CO at 531.9 eV and C–O at 533.3 eV [42], coincidental with FTIR studies. In brief, CDs include a wide variety of N- and O-containing groups. These groups conduce to not noly improving the dispersion stability of CDs, but also enhancing the cement hydration [44], thus endowing CDs with a promising potential for promoting the properties of cementitious materials.

The inherent optical property of CDs is determined using UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3a shows that CDs in aqueous solution exhibit two absorption peaks at 244 and 336 nm, ascribed to π−π* transitions of sp2-carbon network and n–π* transitions of O-/N-containing groups, respectively [45]. Additionally, the aqueous CD solution appears yellow under sunlight but emits noticeable blue fluorescence under the excitation of a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3a, inset). As illustrated in PL spectra (Fig. 3b), CDs display an excitation-independent PL behavior with the excitation/emission maximum at 380/452 nm. In a word, CDs exhibit distinctive optical performance, providing a novel method to evaluate their dispersion stability within simulated concrete pore solution.

To sum up, all the characterization above demonstrates that low-cost CDs prepared by facile and rapid microwave treatment possess a small average size of 1.24 nm, an excellent monodispersity, a favorable crystalline structure, and abundant O-/N-containing surface groups. These characteristics, particularly multitudes of surface groups, are expected to render CDs to enhance the properties of cementitious materials.

As is well-known, great dispersion stability is one of the crucial prerequisites for nanomaterials to effectively exert their positive effects in cementitious materials [16]. Taking advantage of CDs intrinsic optical properties, UV–vis spectra and PL spectra (Fig. 4 ) are adopted to validate CDs dispersion stability within simulated concrete pore solution. In general, the PL of CDs will be impaired, and even eliminated when they agglomerate due to their aggregation-induced quenching effect [46]. As shown in the inset of Fig. S2, there is no visible precipitate in different solutions containing CDs after 7-day standing. And with the concentration of CDs increasing, the solution shows more appreciable yellow color under daylight. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of the solution under 365 nm UV irradiation gradually enhances as the increase of CDs content. This qualitatively demonstrates that CDs are well dispersed without agglomeration in simulated concrete pore solution regardless of concentrations, and exhibit a superior long-term dispersion stability. As illustrated in Fig. 4a and b, the peak intensities of UV–vis and maximum PL spectroscopy progressively increase with augmenting the dosage of CDs. Crucially, the peak intensities of UV–vis and maximum PL spectroscopy from CDs-containing simulated concrete pore solution are approximately consistent after 7-day sitting (Fig. 4c and d), quantitatively evidencing that CDs hold an excellent long-term dispersion stability. In a word, the qualitative and quantitative analyses simultaneously substantiate that CDs exhibit exceptional long-term dispersion stability within simulated concrete pore solution, thus overcoming a major limitation of nanomaterials (namely, agglomeration) employed in cementitious materials. It should be emphasized that even after one month, there is no visible agglomeration in simulated concrete pore solution containing CDs, testifying that CDs possess a staggering long-term dispersion stability.

For a better understanding of the potential application of this novel and low-cost nanomaterial (i.e., CDs) in cementitious materials, their effects on hydration, mechanical properties and chloride binding capacity of cement are comprehensively evaluated.

The hydration kinetics of diverse cement pastes are uncovered by the isothermal calorimeter. As shown in Fig. 5 a, compared with that of the reference group (i.e., C0 group), the cumulative heat release from cement pastes containing CDs is significantly lower. And with CDs concentration increasing, the cumulative heat release gradually declines. Generally, cumulative heat release is considered as a reliable indicator of the hydration degree at the early stage [47]. Hence, the incorporation of CDs dramatically reduces the early cement hydration degree. Fig. 5b presents the heat flow in various groups. Normally, the moment of maximum hydration heat release can be determined by the second exothermic peak position (namely, at the end of the acceleration period), and this peak value represents the highest rate of hydration [48]. Visibly, as the addition of CDs increases, the hydration process is progressively delayed and the peak value gradually declines. Specifically, C4 group containing 0.2 wt% CDs displays the exothermic peak at 50 h, and the peak value is 0.0012 W/g, demonstrating a 37 h delay and a 75% lower than those of C0 group respectively. In a word, CDs, as a novel low-cost and well dispersed nanomaterial, markedly retard the early hydration of cement pastes ranging 0 from 0.2 wt%.

To evaluate the influence of CDs on the mechanical properties of cement mortars, the compressive strength and flexural strength of various cement mortars are examined after 7-, 14-, and 28-day curing (Fig. 6 ). Evidently, the compressive/flexural strengths (Fig. 6a and b) of all cement mortars consistently increase over curing times. While all CDs-containing samples have less compressive/flexural strength than C0 group when the curing time is no greater than 14 d. And C4 group possesses the smallest compressive/flexural strength regardless of 7- or 14-day curing. This suggests that CDs cannot enhance the mechanical properties of cement mortar at the early stage (less than 14 d), possibly assigned to their retarding effect. In stark contrast, the compressive/flexural strengths of C2 and C3 groups (including 0.05 wt% and 0.1 wt% CDs, respectively) are improved compared with that of C0 group after the curing age is extended to 28 d. And C3 group presents the highest compressive/flexural strength with a 17%/21% improvement. While with reference to C1 and C4 groups, their compressive/flexural strengths are still lower than that of the C0 group after 28-day curing, and C4 group owns the smallest compressive/flexural strength. This finding indicates that CDs can improve the mechanical properties of cement mortars at the late stage (28 d) with their incorporation ranging from 0.05 wt% to 0.1 wt%; But when CDs dosage is 0.025 wt% or 0.2 wt%, the mechanical properties of the cement mortars are impaired.

Chloride binding can effectively transform free chloride to bound chloride through hydration products mainly involving C–S–H and Friedel's salt (Fs), reducing chloride concentration in concrete, thereby contributing to enhancing its durability [49]. To get insight into the impact of CDs on the chloride binding capacity of cement materials, chloride binding content (C b) values of various pastes after immersing in NaCl solution at different periods are determined employing the typical equilibrium test. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the C b values of all groups visibly improve as the immersion time prolongs. And the addition of CDs notably increases C b values at any immersion time. Distinctly, with the increasing of CDs content, C b values share a rising tendency regardless of immersion time. Thus, C4 group exhibits the greatest C b values of 48.26 mg/g after immersing for 28 days, demonstrating a 51% increase compared with C0 group. It is acknowledged that the agglomeration of nanomaterials tends to diminish their positive effects on improving chloride binding actions of cement materials. The observation above reflects the excellent dispersity of CDs within cement pastes. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7b, at 1-day immersion, all the CDs-containing samples possess the highest increase rates of C b values irrespective of CDs dosage. Obviously, C4 group reveals the highest C b increase rate, reaching an impressive 112%. This testifies that CDs significantly accelerate the chloride binding of cement pastes at the initial immersion stage. With the immersion time extending, the increase rates of C b in almost all groups gradually decrease regardless of CDs concentration, implying that the enhancement of CDs on chloride binding diminishes over immersion times. Overall, CDs exhibit a significant enhancement in the chloride binding capacity of cement pastes.

In brief, in sharp contrast to other nanomaterials including GO, nano-SiO2, and CNTs [4,10–12], CDs possess entirely different effects on hydration kinetics and mechanical properties of cementitious materials. As for hydration kinetics, previous literature demonstrated that nano-SiO2 can significantly accelerate the early hydration of cement [4]. However, CDs significantly retard cement hydration rather than promoting it, and the retardation effect gradually increases with increasing CDs content. Regarding mechanical properties, according to a previous literature, CNTs enhance the mechanical properties of cementitious materials at 7-, 14-, 28-day curing with an optimal dosage of 0.1–0.2 wt% [50]. Nonetheless, CDs tend to compromise the mechanical properties of cement mortars when the curing time is no more than 14 d; While after the curing time reaches 28 d, CDs improve the mechanical properties of mortars with a dosage ranging from 0.05 wt% to 0.1 wt%, but still diminish the mechanical properties as their content is 0.025 wt% or 0.2 wt%. With respect to chloride binding, in a previous study, the incorporation of 0.2 wt% GO increased the C b values of cement paste by only 46% [51]. Nevertheless, cement pastes containing 0.2 wt% CDs improved the C b values by 51% under identical experimental conditions. These findings above suggest that this novel low-cost and well dispersed CDs can act as a retarder to delay cement hydration at the early stage, and synchronously function as a nanoreinforcement to improve mechanical and anti-chloride abilities of cementitious materials. This conduces to solving problems of high cost and poor dispersity of existing nanomaterials employed in nano-engineering, and established the strong foundation for future applications of novel CDs in cement composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase compositions and microstructures of CDs-containing cement composites
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely known that the phase compositions and microstructures of cement composites strongly correlate with their mechanics and chloride binding ability. Therefore, the phase composition and microstructure of cement pastes containing CDs are fully investigated.

Typically, the main ingredients of cement hydration products involve C–S–H, Ca(OH)2 (CH), and ettringite/monosulfate (AFt/Ms). And CaCO3 (Cc) is the carbonization product unavoidably produced by the carbonation reaction between CO2 and CH. The crucial details about phase compositions of paste samples are disclosed through XRD and TGA/DTG analyses. Fig. 8 a and b illustrate the XRD patterns of diverse paste samples before and after immersion in NaCl solution for 28 days, and the corresponding phase contents are analyzed using the Rietveld method (Fig. 8c and d). As shown in Fig. 8a, all the paste samples exhibit similar peaks, including CH (peaks at 18.1°, 34°, and 47.1°), Cc (peaks at 29.4°), and some unhydrated products (peaks at 11.7°, 32.3°, and 32.7°, attributed to Gypsum (Gyp), C3S, and C2S respectively). This suggests that CDs fail to change the types of cement hydration products. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the contents of Gyp, C2S, and C3S gradually decline with the increase in CDs dosage. On the contrary, the C–S–H content shows an upward tendency. This observation indicates that CDs significantly promote cement hydration after 28-day curing, thus decreasing the content of unhydrated products, and generating more C–S–H. In detail, C4 group presents the highest C–S–H content of 57.38%, enhancing by 13.06% compared with C0 group (50.75%). This conduces to enhancing the later mechanical properties and physically adsorbed chloride ions of cementitious materials. Differently, after 28-day immersion in NaCl solution, the new peaks at 11°, 22°, and 23° are assigned to Fs. Since Fs is formed by the reaction between chloride ions and Ms, chloride chemical binding occurs (Fig. 8b) [49]. More importantly, the main diffraction peak intensity of Fs at 11.7° increases with the addition of CDs, suggesting that CDs can enhance the chloride chemical binding capacity of cement pastes. It should be pointed out that the intensity of CH peak at 18.1° is considerably lower, consistent with observations in previous work [52]. This is probably ascribed to the participation of CH in the formation of Fs [53]. With respect to Fig. 8d, unhydrated products gradually reduce in all the samples, indicating that they are constantly consumed by hydration reaction during 28-day immersion. Meanwhile, as CDs dosage increases, the content of unhydrated products declines; While the C–S–H content increases progressively from 53.09% for C0 group to 62.87% for C4 group. This demonstrates that CDs can further promote the generation of C–S–H even in the NaCl solution, thus enhancing the chloride physical adsorption. With respect to Fs, the addition of CDs also improve the generation of Fs, and Fs content continuously increased with the CDs dosage increasing. Specifically, Fs content increases from 8.57% for C0 group to 10.31% for C4 group. Apparently, the increment of Fs induced by CDs is lower than that of C–S–H, inferring that CDs significantly promote physical adsorption, and relatively limitedly improve chemical binding of chloride in cement pastes.

TGA/DTG (Fig. 9 ) analysis is conducted to attain more specific information related to the contents of hydration products in cement pastes. Before immersion in the NaCl solution (Fig. 9a), all samples exhibit identical mass loss peaks at specific temperature ranges, including 50–150 °C, 150–200 °C, 410–500 °C, and 600–800 °C, associating with the decomposition of C–S–H/AFt, Ms, CH, and Cc, respectively [54,55]. This emphasizes that CD unable to modify the type of hydration product, aligning with XRD results. It should be highlighted that C–S–H has a considerably higher mass ratio than AFt, so the mass loss peak at the range of 50–150 °C predominantly belongs to C–S–H [35,44]. Evidently, C–S–H from CDs-incorporated samples exhibit relatively stronger weight loss peaks than the reference group (i.e., C0 group), and the higher the dosage of CDs, the more the content of C–S–H. This further indicates CDs facilitate the formation of C–S–H, thereby conducing to improving the later mechanical properties and chloride physical adsorption of cement composites. In contrast, in all the samples after immersing in the NaCl solution, a new weight loss peak attributed to Fs appears at 250–370 °C [56], and the mass loss peak of Ms significantly increases (Fig. 9b). This suggests that Ms is further generated after immersion; Meanwhile, chloride chemical binding occurs due to the formation of Fs. Furthermore, the mass loss peaks of Fs and Ms in CDs-containing pastes are marginally higher than those of the reference group, and their contents progressively increase with the rising of CDs dosage. Specifically, C4 group shows the highest Fs and Ms content. This suggests that CDs promote more Fs and Ms generation to increase the chloride chemical binding, but the improvement is unconspicuous, in line with the inference of XRD-Rietveld. In terms of C–S–H, their content in CD-incorporated samples rises as the dosage of CDs augments. Among them, C4 group presents the maximum C–S–H content, suggesting that incorporating more CDs facilitates the production of more C–S–H, thus more efficiently promoting chloride physical absorption. While for later mechanical properties of cement composites, there is no linear relation between CDs dosage and mechanical properties, probably because they are also apparently affected by microstructures besides C–S–H content.

SEM and MIP are utilized to investigate the effect of CDs on the microstructure of cement composites. Fig. 10 presents SEM images of various samples. After 28-day curing, C0 group displays numerous unhydrated particles (with a size of 6–8 μm) irregularly attached by a small quantity of amorphous gel-like C–S–H (Fig. 10a). With the addition of CDs, unhydrated particles are further dissolved to smaller sizes of 3–4 μm, and their surfaces are covered by more C–S–H (Fig. 10b). Regarding C4 group (Fig. 10c), the size of unhydrated particles decreases to 1–2 μm, and almost all of their surfaces are completely covered with more C–S–H. Compared with C0 and C2 groups, C4 group visibly possesses more C–S–H, and a progressively denser distribution. This indicates that CDs promote clinker dissolution, and form more C–S–H after 28-day curing, and the more the incorporation of CDs, the more significant their promotion effect. This well coincides with the findings from XRD and TGA/DTG. Moreover, CDs with high dispersity can modulate the distribution of C–S–H, enabling it denser and more homogeneous. With respect to the samples following 28-day of immersion in the NaCl solution, it is difficult to find unhydrated particles; While large-sized hexagonal CH sheets with a dimension of 1–3 μm, amorphous gel-like C–S–H, and hexagonal-like lamellar Fs crystals are observed (Fig. 10d-f). In terms of C0 group (Fig. 10d), a number of C–S–H is haphazardly attached to CH sheets, and Fs are sparsely distributed. After incorporating CDs (Fig. 10e), more C–S–H is evenly distributed on the surfaces of CH sheets, and Fs crystals distribute uniformly, displaying a flower-like construction. Regarding C4 group (Fig. 10f), the amount of C–S–H exhibits an evident increase and entirely covers CH sheets. This further substantiates that highly dispersed CDs promote the formation of more C–S–H, and make it uniformly distribute, thus leading to stronger chloride physical adsorption. Meanwhile, CDs enhance Fs generation, and improve their distribution from random to homogeneous flower-like configuration, thereby conducing to boosting chloride chemical binding. Additionally, these CDs-caused changes contribute to promoting the enhancement of later mechanics performance.

Since the pore structures of cement mortars are highly associated with their mechanical properties, MIP is employed to disclose exhaustive information about their pore structures. As shown in Fig. 11 a, the reference group (i.e. C0 group) displays the smallest cumulative pore volume among all the groups. This demonstrates that different from other nanomaterials such as GO and CNTs [10–12], CDs with abundant surface groups serving as a retarder cause air entrainment [57], thus enlarging the cumulative pore volume of mortars. It tends to exert a negative effect on mechanical properties. As for CDs-containing samples, C2 and C3 groups share smaller cumulative pore volumes than C4 and C1 groups, suggesting that suitable addition of CDs (ranging from 0.05 to 0.1 wt%) can decrease the cumulative pore volume, possible since the CDs-induced cement hydration in C2 and C3 groups more markedly counteract the negative effect of air entrainment. This is primarily responsible for the lower mechanical properties of C1 and C4 groups, and higher mechanical properties of C2 and C3 groups than C0 group. Fig. 11b depicts the pore size distribution of various mortar samples. Among them, C0 group shows the most frequent pore diameter of 258.91 nm, larger than that of CDs-containing samples. This is potentially due to its insufficient cement hydration, resulting in defects of pore structures. As for CD-containing mortars, the most frequent pore diameter decreases with increasing CD dosage. Specifically, C3 group exhibits the smallest the most frequent pore diameter of 21.46 nm, followed by C2 (31.15 nm), C1 (40.59 nm), and C4 (50.42 nm), drastically decreasing by 92.0%, 88.0%, 84.4%, 80.6% than C0 group respectively. This infers that owing to their hydration promotion, CDs can refine the pore structures of mortars, leading to smaller the most frequent pore diameters; While with the further increasing of CDs incorporation to 0.2 wt%, the most frequent pore diameters of mortars gradually augment on account of CDs-caused air entrainment.

In conclusion, the phase compositions, and microstructures of all the samples substantiate that CDs with high dispersity significantly promote the generation and uniformization of C–S–H and Fs regardless of curing for 28 days or further immersing in NaCl solution for 28 days. And the more the incorporation of CDs, the better the promotion. This conduces to the improvement of mechanical properties and chloride binding capacity of cement composites. At the same time, CDs are able to refine the pore structures of cement matrices, thus decreasing the most frequent pore diameter. In sharp contrast to other nanomaterials such as GO and CNTs, CDs enlarge the cumulative pore volume due to CDs-induced air entrainment, playing a negative role in the mechanical properties of cement composites. More seriously, when the content of CDs reaches 0.2 wt%, the proportion of more harmful pores is inclined to increase, thereby further compromising mechanical properties. This explains the reason that there is no linear relation between CDs dosage and mechanical properties of cementitious materials.

Low-cost and high-dispersion CDs exert different effects on cement composites in stark contrast to other nanomaterials. In detail, they have a negetive effect on the hydration kinetics, a positive impact on chloride binding capacity, and a complicated influence on mechanical properties of cementitious materials. The phase compositions and microstructures of various cement matrices can provide an insight into the related mechanisms. Thus, this section will comprehensively reveal these mechanisms in light of all the studies above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of hydration kinetics retardation induced by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of previous studies, the characterization of CDs, and hydration heat experiments, the mechanism of hydration kinetics retardation from CDs-modified cement is rationally elucidated (Fig. 12 ). That is, CDs with lots of O-/N-containing groups create a protective barrier to retard hydration through the complexation of their abundant surface groups with Ca2+. Firsly, in the previous work, citric acid with a multitude of carboxyl (–COOH) dramatically retards cement hydration proved by the dissolved organic carbon measurement, Zeta potential, and the thermodynamic calculations [58–60]. In detail, citric acid can be adsorbed on the surface of cement particles and rapidly removed from the pore solution, and there are no crystalline citrate precipitated in the solution [58,60]. Hence, citric acid adsorbs onto the surface of clinkers by the reaction between –COOH and Ca2+, thereby forming a protective layer around the cement particles to prevent the nucleation, growth, and precipitation of hydration products. Meanwhile, CDs generally possess hereditability (i.e., inheriting certain physicochemical features from precursors) [61,62]. In this work, the precursors for CDs synthesis involve citric acid, thus endowing CDs with similar physicochemical characteristics of citric acid. The FTIR and XPS spectra (Fig. 2) further substantiate abundant O-containing groups (particularly –COOH) on CDs surface. More importantly, the isothermal calorimeter testing (Fig. 5) demonstrates that the addition of CDs significantly decreases cumulative heat releases and peak values of heat flow curves, and simultaneously delays exothermic peak at the end of the acceleration period. Hence, CDs retard the early hydration of the cement system. And the more the introduction of CDs, the more serious the retardation of early cement hydration. Therefore, CDs with numerous –COOH groups are supposed to exert an identical effect with citric acid on hydration kinetics. In addition, it should be noted that amides formed by the condensation reaction of citric acid and urea on CDs surface (determined by FTIR and XPS in Fig. 2) can also contribute to retarding cement hydration. Specifically, Ca2+ complexes with N atoms of the amide to form ligands [63,64], reducing the concentration of Ca2+ released during hydration, thereby retarding the cement hydration process [64]. Overall, CDs possess a large number of O-/N-containing groups including –COOH and amides groups. These groups can complexes with Ca2+ of cement particles, forming a protective layer on their surface, thus significantly retarding cement hydration kinetics at the early stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of mechanical properties affected by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to compressive/flexural strength tests, XRD, TGA/DTG, SEM, and MIP, the mechanism for mechanical properties of mortars affected by CDs are reasonably proposed (Fig. 13 ). Namely, CDs carry out nucleation effect, accelerating hydration, and refining pore structure to improve mechanical behaviors; And CDs synchronously exhibit retarding and air-entraining effects, deteriorating mechanical properties. These above effects simultaneously affect mechanical properties, leading to a complex relationship between CDs dosage and mechanical properties. In other words, the mechanical properties of mortars are simultaneously influenced by nucleation, retarding, and air-entraining effects of CDs. Specifically, it is notoriously believed that the mechanical properties of cementitious materials highly depend on the degree of microstructure refinement and their hydration product (mainly C–S–H) content [65,66]. As for CDs, their effect on the mechanical properties of mortars is variational with the cement hydration evolution. At the early stage (before 14-day curing), CDs play a negative role in the mechanical properties irrespective of CDs dosage. This is attributed to the retarding effect of CDs (certified by isothermal calorimeter in Fig. 5), leading to inadequate hydration degree. Meanwhile, retarders with plentiful surface groups are normally air-entraining [57], resulting in multitudes of pores within cement matrices. Thus, CDs as a retarder also implement air-entraining effects to negatively affect the mechanical actions. More seriously, CDs fail to execute the nucleating effect, since CDs-induced retardation brings about insufficient Ca2+ released during cement dissolution. Thus, CD-containing mortars share lower mechanical behaviors than the reference group (substantiated by the compressive/flexural strength test in Fig. 6) before 14-day curing. Regarding the later stage (after 28-day curing), CDs with a dosage of 0.05 wt% and 0.1 wt% play a positive role in the mechanical properties; While with the addition of 0.025 wt% and 0.2 wt%, CDs exert a negative effect on mechanical properties. In terms of samples after 28-day curing, CDs provide nucleation sites to enhance cement hydration, thus promoting more C–S–H formation and rendering it uniformly distributed. And with their incorporation increasing, CDs-caused improvement is more effective (substantiated by XRD, TGA/DTG, SEM in Fig. 8, 9 and 10). Meanwhile, the introduction of CDs also decreases the most frequent pore diameter (demonstrated by MIP in Fig. 11b). These CDs-caused changes are beneficial to promoting the mechanical actions of cement materials. Nevertheless, the cumulative pore volumes of CDs-containing samples increase due to CDs-induced air entrainment. With reference to C2 and C3 groups, the nucleating effect of CDs counteracts the detrimental influence of their air-entraining effect, thus improving the mechanical properties of mortars; While C1 and C4 groups, the air-entraining effects of CDs are more significant, resulting in higher cumulative pore volumes than C2 and C3 groups (determined by MIP in Fig. 11a), thereby compromising mechanical properties (certified by compressive/flextual strength test in Fig. 6). Additionally, it should be pointed out that after 28-day curing, retarding effect of CDs has a negligible influence on mechanical properties of mortars. Consequently, in stark contrast to other nanomaterials including GO and CNTs, CDs simultaneously display nucleating, air-entraining and retarding effects on mortars, thereby owning a complicated effect on their mechanical properties. It must be emphasized that the positive effect of CDs on the later mechanical properties of cement systems can be achieved by adjusting their incorporation. This enables low-cost and highly dispersed CDs to serve as a novel nanoreinforcement to improve the later mechanical behaviors of cementitious materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs promoting chloride binding
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism of improving the chloride binding capacity of CD-incorporated cement pastes is elaborately proposed. Namely, incorporating CDs significantly amplifies C–S–H generation and marginally promotes Fs formation, which considerably augments the chloride physical adsorption and accompanies a negligible facilitation of chloride chemical binding. Specifically, It is generally accepted that C–S–H not only affects the mechanical properties, but also is predominately responsible for physical adsorption of free chloride in cement matrices. While chloride chemical binding is predominated by Fs [49]. Apart from C–S–H and Fs, the impact of other phases on the chloride binding capacity is insignificant [67]. To shed light on the distribution of bound chloride content in Fs and C–S–H, the calculations of chemically bound chloride (
C b Fs
) attributing to Fs and physically adsorbed chloride (
C b CSH
) resulting from C–S–H are conducted according to Eq. (3) and (4) (shown in Supplementary Information). The calculation result (Fig. 14 ) manifests that CDs-containing groups tremendously enhance
C b CSH
values and marginally increase
C b Fs
values compared with C0 group. As the CDs dosage increases, both
C b CSH
and
C b Fs
values also increase, and the
C b CSH
value rises more remarkably. Thus, C4 group displays the greatest
C b CSH
and
C b Fs
values, increasing by 89% and 14% compared with C0 group respectively. This suggests that CDs enhance the chloride binding capacity of cement pastes mainly by promoting chloride physical adsorption.

More specifically, as shown in Fig. 15 , the amount of C–S–H is insufficient in the reference group due to incomplete cement hydration. Correspondingly, Ms is also lacking, unable to convert sufﬁcient amounts of Fs through ion exchange. This brings out that the physical adsorption and chemical binding of chloride are weak. After incorporating CDs into cement pastes, CDs possess nucleation effects, enhancing the generation of C–S–H and Fs [68]. More importantly, CDs exhibit an ultra-small particle size (ca. 1.24 nm), and their surface presents numerous N-/O- containing surface groups (certified by TEM, FTIR, and XPS in Figs. 1 and 2), contributing to the improvement of cement hydration [44]. Taking advantage of these characteristics, a multitude of high-density C–S–H is generated (substantiated by SEM in Fig. 10) in CDs-containing pastes, thus manifesting a notable positive influence on chloride physical adsorption. In addition to promoting C–S–H formation, CDs can also facilitate the formation of more Fs (certified by XRD and TGA/DTG in Figs. 8 and 9). Nonetheless, this promotion is limited. Consequently, CDs slightly enhance the chloride chemical binding of cementitious materials. Overall, at the range of 0–0.2 wt%, when the dosage of CDs increases, the chloride binding performance of cementitious materials is remarkably enhanced.

In conclusion, this work first investigates the effect of a novel low-cost and high-dispersity carbon-based nanomaterial (i.e., CDs) on hydration kinetics, mechanical properties, and chloride binding capacity of cementitious materials. More importantly, the relevant mechanisms are discussed in detail. The major conclusions are as follows.
(1)
CDs are successfully synthesized by a simple, time-saving, and eco-friendly microwave route only using non-toxic citric acid and urea as precursors. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit small size of ca. 1.24 nm, outstanding crystallinity, and abundant O-/N-containing groups. More importantly, CDs are cost-efficiency with a staggering cost of $0.013/g, and possess a remarkable long-term dispersion stability within the simulated concrete pore solution for 7 d. These characteristics endow CDs with the ability to overcome the two major problems of existing nanomaterials: high cost and poor dispersity.
(2)
CDs significantly retard the early cement hydration kinetics, improve the later mechanical performance, and remarkably enhance the chloride binding. Specifically, the more the incorporation of CDs, the more retarded the hydration kinetics, but the better the chloride binding. Incorporating 0.2 wt% CDs dramatically reduces the second exothermic peak value by 75%, but obviously improves the chloride binding capacity of cement paste by 51%. Differently, there is a complex relationship between CDs dosage and mechanical properties of mortars. CDs play a negative effect on the early (before 14-day curing) mechanical performance regardless of dosages; While CDs with suitable incorporation between 0.05 and 0.1 wt% promote the 28-day mechanical properties of mortars. The addition of 0.1 wt% CDs enhances the compressive/flexural strength of mortar by 17%/21%.
(3)
The observations of XRD, TGA/DTG, SEM, and MIP substantiate that CDs with high dispersity significantly promote the generation and homogenization of hydration products (C–S–H and Fs), regardless of 28-day curing or further 28-day immersion in NaCl solution. And the higher the CDs dosage, the more the C–S–H and Fs. Thus, CDs refine the pore structures of cement matrices, thereby decreasing the most frequent pore diameter. However, distinctly different from existing nanomaterials such as GO and CNTs, CDs enlarge the cumulative pore volume due to CDs-induced air entrainment.
(4)
According to the phase composition and microstructure analyses of various samples, the related influence mechanism of CDs on cement systems is reasonably described. In detail, the CDs-induced retarding mechanism is that CDs with abundant N-/O-containing groups create a protective barrier to retard hydration through the complexation of their abundant surface groups with Ca2+; The mechanism of mechanical properties affected by CDs is that the mechanical performance of mortars is simultaneously influenced by nucleation, retarding, and air-entraining effects of CDs, leading to an intricate relationship between them and CDs concentration; The mechanism of improving chloride binding performance by CDs is that CDs remarkably facilitate C–S–H generation and marginally improve Fs formation, resulting in significant enhancement of chloride physical adsorption and negligible promotion of chloride chemical binding.

These results above substantiate that low-cost and well dispersed CDs can be employed to be a retarder to delay cement hydration, and simultaneously a nanoreinforcement to enhance mechanical and anti-chloride capacities of cement composites. This work provides a better understanding of the influence of CDs on the properties of cementitious materials, and would advance their nano-engineering application, thus stimulating the development of high-performance cement matrices.
